questions.question: What is the primary purpose of rate limiting?
options: To make responses load faster
To cap how much of a service any single client can consume, protecting availability and fairness
To encrypt client requests
To cache responses
questions.explanation: Rate limiting caps requests per client per time window so no single caller — malicious, buggy, or greedy — can exhaust the service and harm everyone. It protects availability, enforces fairness, and supports tiered/monetized APIs.

questions.question: What is the edge-case bug with a naive fixed-window counter ('100 per minute, reset at the boundary')?
options: It can never allow any requests
A client can send 100 just before the boundary and 100 just after — ~200 in a couple seconds, double the intended rate
It uses too much memory
It blocks all clients equally
questions.explanation: Because the counter resets at fixed clock boundaries, requests clustered around the boundary (end of one window + start of the next) can reach 2x the limit in a short span — often at the worst moment. Token bucket and sliding window avoid this.

questions.question: How does the token-bucket algorithm handle a client that has been idle?
options: It blocks them for being idle
Their bucket has refilled to capacity, so they can make a burst of requests up to the bucket size before being throttled to the refill rate
It gives them unlimited requests forever
It resets their limit to zero
questions.explanation: Tokens accumulate up to the capacity while idle. So a quiet client has a full bucket and can fire a burst (up to capacity) immediately, then is limited to the steady refill rate. This matches real bursty traffic, which is why token bucket is so widely used.

questions.question: What status code should a rate limiter return when a client exceeds its limit?
options: 404 Not Found
429 Too Many Requests, ideally with a Retry-After header
500 Internal Server Error
200 OK with empty body
questions.explanation: HTTP 429 Too Many Requests signals the client has exceeded its rate limit. A Retry-After header tells well-behaved clients when to try again so they can back off (Lesson 02), while the limiter shields the backend from the rest.

questions.question: Why does running an independent rate limiter on each of 4 gateway instances fail to enforce a global limit?
options: The instances crash
Each instance allows the full limit, so a client spread across them gets up to 4x the intended global limit — the counter must be shared (e.g. Redis)
Rate limiting can't run on a gateway
It actually works perfectly
questions.explanation: If each of N instances independently allows, say, 100/min, a client whose requests land across all of them gets 100N/min total. To enforce a single global limit, the counter must be shared across instances with atomic operations — the distributed rate limiter of Phase 8.
